items.q: Did the New Deal end the Great Depression?
items.correct: It eased unemployment and restored confidence but did not end the Depression — WWII spending did. Roosevelt's reforms reshaped American government permanently.
items.misconception: Roosevelt's New Deal ended the Great Depression by 1936.
items.random_wrong: The New Deal was a treaty with Britain to share gold reserves.
items.why: Unemployment stayed high until war mobilisation in 1941. Students credit FDR's programmes with the full recovery; historians stress that war ended the slump.

items.q: Why is Magna Carta still cited in 'Power and the People' history?
items.correct: Because clauses on lawful judgement and protection from arbitrary imprisonment were reinterpreted in the 17th century to support broader liberties, even though they originally protected only barons.
items.misconception: Magna Carta directly created modern human rights in 1215.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta gave the right to vote to all English men.
items.why: Its symbolic power grew over time, especially during the 17th-century constitutional struggles. Students confuse historical use with original meaning — a key historiographical insight.

items.q: Why did public health reforms accelerate after 1848?
items.correct: The 1848 Public Health Act followed cholera epidemics and Chadwick's report; John Snow's 1854 Broad Street pump study reinforced the case for clean water by 1875.
items.misconception: Public health acts were passed because Victorians discovered germs in the 1840s.
items.random_wrong: The 1875 Act made the NHS free for all workers.
items.why: Germ theory came later (Pasteur, Koch). 1848 reforms were driven by miasma theory plus pragmatic statistics, not bacteriology — students compress the discovery sequence.
The answer examiners want is: Poll tax. “Window tax” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who led the peasants from Kent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Wat Tyler. “John Ball” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Peasants' Revolt?
items.why: The real figure is 1381. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
